coredata.dc:title: Mixed matrix membranes incorporated with Ln-MOF for selective and sensitive detection of nitrofuran antibiotics based on inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Feng
Yao, Hua
Zhao, Yifan
Li, Xing
Zhang, Gaowei
Yang, Yangyi
coredata.dc:description: The detection of antibiotics is critical and challenging due to the pervasive use of antibiotics had inevitably brought some negative impacts on ecosystem and human health. Herein, we reported an anti-interfere ability enhanced luminescent sensor of lanthanide metal–organic framework (Ln-MOF) filled mixed matrix membranes (MMMs), which combining the processibility of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) polymer with Ln-MOF of {[Tb2(AIP)2(H2O)10]·(AIP)·4H2O}n (Tb-AIP, where AIP is 5-aminoisophthalate) fillers. The as-fabricated Tb-AIP MMMs are stable in water with a wide pH range and exhibit characteristic blue emission of Tb3+. Significantly, the Tb-AIP MMMs show highly selective and sensitive to nitrofuran antibiotics (NFAs) via inner filter effect (IFE), and yet remain unaffected not only by other common antibiotics but also by other types of analytes (metal ions and anions) that may coexist. The limits of detection for nitrofurantoin (NFT) and nitrofurazone (NFZ) are 0.30 and 0.35μM, respectively. As a proof of concept, the proposed MMMs sensor are demonstrated to be feasible for application in detecting NFAs in original water of Pearl River and bovine serum samples, the corresponding quenching constants and limits of detection are similar to their standard detections in an acceptable range. Furthermore, this MMMs sensor for NFAs detection was reversible after washing with deionized water. The luminescent Ln-MOF filled MMMs presented here provides a functional platform for simple yet useful in sensing of NFAs in environment and biology systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mixed matrix membranes
Lanthanide metal–organic framework
Sensor
Inner filter effect
Antibiotics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Tb-AIP mixed matrix membranes
The stability of the as-prepared Tb-AIP MMMs
Detection of NFAs in real specimen

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Laboratory Open Fund Project of Sun Yat-Sen University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51472275 and 91022012), Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (2014A030313207) and Laboratory Open Fund Project of Sun Yat-Sen University (20160207).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photos of the as-prepared Tb-AIP MMMs with different loadings of MOFs fillers, (a) under daylight; (b) under UV-light irradiation of 365nm.
The PXRD patterns of Tb-AIP MOF powder, PMMA and MMMs with different loadings.
The SEM measurements of Tb-AIP MOF powder (a), blank PMMA membrane (b), with MOF loadings of 9.1% (c) and 28.6% (d).
UV–vis absorption spectra of different antibiotics with the concentration of 50.0μM and excitation spectrum of Tb-AIP MMMs.
Luminescence changes of Tb-AIP MMMs in addition of different concentrations of NFZ (a) and NFT (c); Stern–Volmer plot for the response to NFZ (b) and NFT (d).
Fluorescent response of Tb-AIP MMMs towards the other competing antibiotics (0.2mM) or a mixture of competing antibiotics (0.2mM) and NFT.
Reusability of the Eu-BCA thin film implemented with 0.2mM of NFT aqueous solution.
Schematic illustration for the preparation Tb-AIP MMMs.
The estimated life-cycle of antibiotics in the environment.
Determination of NFT in practical samples of bovine serum (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Mixed matrix membranes incorporated with Ln-MOF for selective and sensitive detection of nitrofuran antibiotics based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detection of antibiotics is critical and challenging due to the pervasive use of antibiotics had inevitably brought some negative impacts on ecosystem and human health. Herein, we reported an anti-interfere ability enhanced luminescent sensor of lanthanide metal–organic framework (Ln-MOF) filled mixed matrix membranes (MMMs), which combining the processibility of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) polymer with Ln-MOF of {[Tb2(AIP)2(H2O)10]·(AIP)·4H2O}n (Tb-AIP, where AIP is 5-aminoisophthalate) fillers. The as-fabricated Tb-AIP MMMs are stable in water with a wide pH range and exhibit characteristic blue emission of Tb3+. Significantly, the Tb-AIP MMMs show highly selective and sensitive to nitrofuran antibiotics (NFAs) via inner filter effect (IFE), and yet remain unaffected not only by other common antibiotics but also by other types of analytes (metal ions and anions) that may coexist. The limits of detection for nitrofurantoin (NFT) and nitrofurazone (NFZ) are 0.30 and 0.35μM, respectively. As a proof of concept, the proposed MMMs sensor are demonstrated to be feasible for application in detecting NFAs in original water of Pearl River and bovine serum samples, the corresponding quenching constants and limits of detection are similar to their standard detections in an acceptable range. Furthermore, this MMMs sensor for NFAs detection was reversible after washing with deionized water. The luminescent Ln-MOF filled MMMs presented here provides a functional platform for simple yet useful in sensing of NFAs in environment and biology systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been successfully developed for improving human health, and also for preventing and treating animals and plants infections as well as for promoting growth in animal farming [1]. Among the most commonly used broad-spectrum antibiotics, nitrofuran antibiotics (NFAs) were once used in food-producing animals to prevent the infection of intestinal tract bacteria [2], which are only partially metabolized and are then discharged along the excreta. The common practice of discharging livestock waste into rivers or using it as fertilizer on agricultural land has led to the presence of antibiotics in the environment [3]. The residual antibiotics in the environment could generate antimicrobial resistance and other adverse health effects on non-target organisms, contamination of food and drinking water supplies [4]. In order to guarantee the quality and safety of water and food, many countries set strict tolerance levels for these compounds and perform surveillance programs. Unfortunately, NFAs residual are still found frequently in food products, also in both surface water and groundwater as well as in drinking water, NFAs continue to be of regulatory concern. Several analytical methods have been described for the determination of NFAs residues such as gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS), liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC–MS), micellar electrokinetic capillary chromatography (EC) have been reported [5–7]. Although these methods fulfill the sensitivity requirement but have limitations in terms of the costs incurred by the expensive equipment, and are also very often time-consuming [8]. Thus the specific, sensitive, easy-to-use and cost-effective detecting devices have to be developed to monitor the presence of NFAs in water and food. To date, fluorescence detection based chemical sensing has proven to be an extremely excellent method to detect analytes fast and efficiently [9–12].

Lanthanide metal–organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) have excellent luminescent behavior with a long lifetime, high color purity and intense sharp emissions in the near-infrared and visible regions compared with conventional fluorescence sensors, such as organic dye molecules [13], gold nanoparticles [14] and quantum dots [15,16]. These advantages make Ln-MOFs suitable for quantitative analysis of bio-samples due to the eliminating of background luminescence by time-resolved luminescence technology. Thus, the design and synthesis of chemical sensors based on Ln-MOFs for metal ions [17–19], anions [20,21], small organic molecules [22–24], et al., have attracted much attention of researchers. However, despite of a lot of Ln-MOFs chemical sensors have been reported so far, there are still some challenges need to be overcome: (1) most of the Ln-MOFs materials are not stable in water (water always play one kind of quenching agents for luminescent MOFs); (2) the luminescent intensity and structure of the majority of Ln-MOFs only stable in a narrow range of pH, the applied range are limited; (3) the size for most of the Ln-MOFs are difficult to reach nanoscale, it is always a time-consuming process to make MOFs powders into suspensions; (4) it is hard to separate the powders from the analytes solution for recycling; (5) most of the reported sensors are always limited to selective sensing of one specific target among the same category analytes, regardless of the interference by other types of pollution. Therefore, exploring the most appreciated and convenient method to solve these problems is urgently needed.

Mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) are composites consisting of filler particles homogeneously embedded in a polymeric matrix. These materials can derive properties from both the polymer and filler components [25,26]. Recently, MOFs have been used as filler particles in MMMs applications, and significant improvements in gas selectivity and permeability have been reported for these MOF-based membranes [25]. However, the MMMs incorporated with Ln-MOFs as luminescent sensors have never reported. In addition, it is worth mentioning that inner filter effect (IFE)-based fluorescent assay has gained increasing interest for applications in the detection of environmentally or biologically important analytes, which results from the absorptions of the excitation and emission lights of the fluorescent substance or both simultaneously by other absorbents in the detection system [27–35]. The greater spectral overlap between the absorbance spectrum of the analytes and the excitation spectrum of sensors, the higher IFE will be produced. Moreover, the IFE-based sensors offers considerable flexibility and simpler to implement without requiring establishing any chemical linking between the fluorophore and the receptor [36,37].

On the basis of above consideration, herein, we report a simple method for luminescent sensor development by using the cost-effective and transparent poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) as the polymer and Ln-MOF of {[Tb2(AIP)2(H2O)10]·(AIP)·4H2O}n (Tb-AIP, where AIP is 5-aminoisophthalate) as the fillers to fabricate luminescent MMMs. As expected, this MMMs sensors are stable in water with a wide pH range, which can be used as a highly effective sensor for detecting NFAs (NFT and NFZ are the representative of NFAs) in experimental deionized water and the original water of Pearl River as well as bovine serum samples. In addition, the MMMs sensors exhibit enhanced anti-interference capability compared with the Tb-AIP powders, and also can be reused in a consecutive testing process. These outstanding performances make the Tb-AIP MMMs a promising platform for real-time and convenient detection of NFAs.

All reagents were of analytical grade, purchased from commercial sources and used directly without further purification. The powder X-ray powder diffraction patterns (PXRD) were collected using a D/MAX 2200VPC diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406Å) at a voltage of 40kV and a current of 26mAcm−2. The morphologies of the films were characterized by an ultra-high resolution field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, SU8010, Hitachi). All the emission spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer. The absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV–vis–NIR Spectrophotometer.

In a modified method for synthesis of MOF crystals, a mixture of 5-Aminoisophthalic acid (0.91g, 5mmol) and CH3COONa (0.82g, 10mmol) was firstly dissolved in ethanol (40mL) and H2O (40mL) at room temperature, then the rare-earth salt Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (2.27g, 5mmol) was added to the above solution, and continue vigorous stirring at room temperature, and a large amount of precipitate was formed. After reaction for 1h, the precipitate was collected by filtration, washed several times with ethanol and water, and dried at 60°C for 2h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Tb-AIP mixed matrix membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic scheme for Tb-AIP MMMs is presented in Scheme 1. Different mass of Tb-AIP MOF powder fillers of 10mg, 25mg, 50mg, 75mg, 100mg and 200mg were respectively added to the PMMA chloroform solution (25mg/mL, 20mL) and then the dispersed suspensions were sonicated for 30min in ice water. The luminescent MMMs with corresponding mass loading of 2.0%, 4.8%, 9.1%, 13.0%, 16.7% and 28.6% were formed by dropping the above mixed slurry on slide glasses, followed by drying in an oven at 40°C overnight. The free standing luminescent sensor was obtained by peeling off the MMMs from slide glass.

The Tb-AIP MMMs was cut into the suitable size for the fluorescence cuvette. Then the scissored MMMs were directly immersed in antibiotics aqueous solution with a series of concentrations. The luminescence response spectrum were collected from 450 to 650nm with the excitation wavelength fixed at 360nm at room temperature. The Tb-AIP MMMs under UV lamp were excited at 365nm.

The real water samples were collected from the Pearl River (the biggest river in South China). The collected water samples were filtered through 0.45µm pore size membrane to remove the suspended particles, the samples were then spiked with known concentration of NFAs (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 90, 110, 150, 200μM) and used for sensing purposes.

The bovine serum was diluted 100 times using deionized water. The bovine serum samples were added with different concentrations of NFAs separately to prepare the spiked samples, the specific concentrations are the same with the spiked concentrations in real water samples. The concentrations of NFT used for recovery test are 15, 25, 35μM, respectively, and the results from three individual experiments were averaged.

It is easy to imagine that the loadings of the fillers have a great influence on morphologies of MMMs. As shown in Fig. 1a, the transparency is reduced with increasing the filler mass, the surface of the MMMs with high MOF loadings of 16.7% and 28.6% were rough, a plenty of MOF particles are aggregated and exposed on the surface. Although the surface of the MMMs with MOF loading of 13.0% is smoother than that of 16.7% and 28.6%, there is still some convex particles can be found. These exposed MOF particles make the MMMs like Tb-AIP MOF powder that will be not stable in water or in a wide pH range either. Thus the high MOF loadings of 13.0%, 16.7% and 28.6% were not the good choice as a sensor. Fig. 1b depicts the luminescent properties of the Tb-AIP MMMs under UV-lamp excited at 365nm, with the increasing of MOF loading, the luminescent intensity increases. Taking all aspects into consideration, obviously, MOF loadings of 9.1% is the optimal one in the selected experiment conditions and used for subsequent experiments.

The XRD patterns of as-prepared Tb-AIP MOF powder, PMMA and the MMMs are shown in Fig. 2. All the diffraction maxima in the pattern of the Tb-AIP MOF powder were identical to those in the simulated PXRD pattern using previously reported data [38]. The X-ray diffraction peaks in the Tb-AIP MMMs contain the peaks of Tb-AIP MOF and PMMA, indicating that the MOF particles remained intact during membrane preparation because of the mild mixing conditions. The morphology and microstructure of the as-prepared materials were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 3a, the Tb-AIP MOF powder samples mostly show blocky structure with length of 3–7µm. Fig. 3b shows the blank PMMA membrane has a smooth surface, and some obvious small protuberances can be found in the MMMs with MOF loadings of 9.1% (Fig. 3c). In contrast, the Tb-AIP MMMs with high loading of 28.6% has a very rough surface, a lot of MOF particles expose to the outside (Fig. 3d). Fluorescence microscopy images of the different MMMs also show the similar results that the more MOF loadings, the more roughened surface (Fig. S1).

The luminescent properties of Tb-AIP MMMs in water at room temperature have been investigated and their excitation and emission spectra are shown in Fig. S2. The excitation spectrum shows a broad bands between 300nm and 390nm with a maximum value at 360nm in their excitation spectra, which are attributed to the overlapping of the π–π* electron transition of the H2AIP ligand. Upon excitation at 360nm, the emission spectrum of Tb-AIP MMMs exhibit four characteristic bands centred at about 488, 543, 585, and 620nm, which are assigned as the 5D4→7F J (J = 6, 5, 4, 3) transitions of the Tb3+ ions. The most prominent characteristic emission peak of Tb-AIP MMMs at 543nm makes it bright green visible under standard UV lamp (excited at 365nm) by the naked eye (Fig. 1b), which is an added advantage for its sensory application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of the as-prepared Tb-AIP MMMs
ce:section.ce:para: Stability is one particularly important property of luminescent sensors, which could avoid false positive/negative results in chemical and biological sensing. Considering water usually plays a role of quenching agent for MOFs based luminescent sensors, the stability of the thin film in water was firstly investigated via time-dependent luminescence intensity analysis of the strongest 5D4→7F5 emission at 543nm. As shown in Fig. S3, although the luminescence intensity of Tb-AIP powder remain basically stable after 5min, it has decreased by approximately 10% compared with its original. However, the intensity of Tb-AIP MMMs remained almost the same in an hour, indicating the excellent water stability. Moreover, most of MOFs are able to survive only in a very narrow pH range, and thus limits their application. The pH stability of the as-prepared Tb-AIP MMMs and powder sample were also investigated. Fig. S4 shows that the luminescence intensity of the Tb-AIP MMMs keeps well in a very broad pH range from 3 to 13, however, the powder sample performs a poor stability. The results demonstrated that the Tb-AIP MMMs can be employed as a good candidate for detecting contaminants in water samples.

In order to evaluate the sensing selectivity of Tb-AIP MMMs, herein, five classes of frequently used antibiotics: nitrofurans (nitrofurazone, NFZ; nitrofurantoin, NFT), nitroimidazoles (metronidazole, MDZ; dimetridazole, DTZ), sulfonamides (sulfadiazine, SDZ; sulfamethazine, SMZ), chloramphenicols (chloramphenicol, CAP; thiamphenicol, THI), and β-lactams (penicillin, PCL) were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4, although different degrees of fluorescence quenching of the suspension were obtained upon the addition of an identical amount of analytes, NFAs exhibit the most significant fluorescence quenching effect. When the concentrations of NFAs were 0.2mM, more than 90% quenching percentage can be observed. The higher quenching efficiency for NFAs compared to the other antibiotics indicates a highly selective response to NFAs. The luminescence of Tb-AIP MMMs can be efficiently quenched by NFAs because of the strong IFE between them. NFAs have strong characteristic absorption at around 370nm in the all antibiotics, and show greater spectral overlap with the excitation spectrum of Tb-AIP MMMs ( Fig. 5).

To more deeply explore the response to NFAs, we investigated the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of NFAs. Fig. 5 shows that the luminescence intensities of Tb-AIP MMMs decreased monotonically and drastically upon the incremental addition of NFZ and NFT ( Fig. 6a and c). When the concentration of NFAs increased to 0.2mM, the quenching efficiency could reach 92% for NFZ and 94% for NFT, estimated based on the luminescence intensity of the 5D4→7F5 transition (located at 543nm). Furthermore, the quenching efficiency can be quantitatively explained using the Stern–Volmer (S–V) equation [39]: I 0/I = K SV[A] + 1, where I 0 and I are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of the analyte, [A] is the molar concentration of the analyte, and K SV is the quenching constant (M−1). The Stern–Volmer plot shown in Fig. 6b and d fit a linear Stern–Volmer equation in the investigated low concentration ranges of 0–60μM. The K sv value is calculated as 2.8 × 104 for NFZ and 4.0 × 104 for NFT, which reveals a strong quenching effect on the Tb-AIP MMMs luminescence. Moreover, there is a good linear relation between the fluorescence intensity and NFAs concentration range from 0 to 40μM (Fig. S5 and S6), and the limits of detection (LOD) for NFZ and NFT are estimated to be 0.35 and 0.30μM, respectively, based on the 3σ IUPAC criteria (3σ/slope).

The anti-interference capability of sensors for selective detection of antibiotics in water system is highly desirable for practical applications. The effects of various coexisting antibiotics on the luminescence intensities of the Tb-AIP MMMs were further investigated by mixing the interfering other antibiotics with NFT ( Fig. 7). It can be seen that the MMMs sensor had similar behavior to that containing pure NFT, indicating that the developed Tb-AIP MMMs sensor showed excellent simultaneously selectivity towards NFAs even in the present of other competitive analytes. In addition, considering the noxious metal ions or anions may coexist in contaminated water and also to further evaluate the anti-interference capability, a comparison between the Tb-AIP MMMs and Tb-AIP powder suspension toward different metal ions or anions were carefully investigated. In this part, the luminescence response of powder suspension with different metal ions and anions were firstly studied. As shown in Fig. S7 and S8, Cu2+, H2PO4 2– and PO4 3– resulted in a significant luminescence quenching of Tb-AIP powder suspension, which means these ions will lead to huge disturbance on the detection of NFAs. In contrast, these three ions have a negligible effect on the luminescence of Tb-AIP MMMs (Fig. S9), and the quenching efficiency of NFAs coexist with these three ions retained almost the same with the original test. All the results disclose the excellent anti-interference capability of Tb-AIP MMMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NFAs in real specimen
ce:section.ce:para: When the antibiotics have become the boon for improving human and animal health, they have also brought many environmental and food safety problems. As shown in Scheme 2, the incorporation of antibiotics in the effluent and sludge from hospitals, factories and livestock farms results in their release to the recipient environments such as surface water and soil. Various antibiotics have been reported in different environmental compartments, such as livestock farms, river water and sediment, soils, and groundwater. All of these antibiotics contaminated substances may affect human health by food chain. The routes A and B that marked in Scheme 2 are the final chances against the threats of antibiotics. Therefore, the detection of NFAs in real water and food are imperative.

Herein, the experiments to investigate the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of Tb-AIP MMMs and concentration of NFAs were firstly carried out in real water sample that collected from the Pearl River, as shown in Fig. S10 and S11, the quenching are mostly similar in nature. The same method is also applied in the detection of NFAs in bovine serum samples (Fig. S16 and S17). As summarized in Table S1, the quenching constants and limits of detection of Tb-AIP MMMs toward NFAs in different environmental samples are very close to their standard detections in an acceptable range. Furthermore, in order to examine the feasibility of Tb-AIP MMMs as a NFAs sensor in complex matrices as well as explore its potential application, the method of standard addition and recovery test was adopted and carried out. As shown in the Table 1, the recovery of NFT is in the range of 94.8–104.4%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 5.0%, indicating that the as-prepared MMMs sensor is promising for the detection of NFT in daily life. The good results of detecting NFAs in real water and bovine serum samples attribute to the excellent stability and anti-interference capability of the Tb-AIP MMMs.

Reusability is a crucial aspect for chemical sensor, based on the above discussion, the as-prepared Tb-AIP MMMs were stable in water and in a wide pH range, which provide a premise for recycle. Compared to the luminescent sensor based on suspension or solution, membranes/thin films have their intrinsic advantages for regeneration. Membranes /Films can be easily recycled by taking them out of the analyte solution directly and washing with an appropriate solvent. The regeneration of the Tb-AIP MMMs were tested by monitoring the fluorescence signal of the film in 0.2mM NFT aqueous solution and blank solution after it was rinsed with water. As shown in Fig. 8, this “ON-OFF-ON” switching process of the MMMs sensor repeated five times, and it has hardly luminescence efficiency loss. The good reusability of Tb-AIP MMMs avoid the situation that random disposal of these pulverous and solutus sensors leads to the reagent consumption and environmental pollution.

In summary, an effective luminescence sensor of Tb-AIP MMMs was developed for sensitive and selective sensing of nitrofuran antibiotics based on IFE quenching. The Tb-AIP MMMs were designed by combining the advantages of the polymers and Ln-MOFs. It's worth noting that this sensor can be prepared in a simple way but with powerful performance such as good stability, enhanced anti-interfere ability and excellent reusability. These outstanding properties make the Tb-AIP MMMs an effective platform for detecting NFAs in real water and bovine serum samples with satisfactory results. Due to the simplicity and sensitivity of analysis, this sensor would have a wide potential application in food and water analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51472275 and 91022012), Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (2014A030313207) and Laboratory Open Fund Project of Sun Yat-Sen University (20160207).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.07.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmental pollution by antibiotics and by antibiotic resistance determinants

Multi-residue monitoring for the simultaneous determination of five nitrofurans (furazolidone, furaltadone, nitrofurazone, nitrofurantoine, nifursol) in poultry muscle tissue through the detection of their five major metabolites (AOZ, AMOZ, SEM, AHD, DNSAH) by liquid chromatography coupled to electrospray tandem mass spectrometry -- in-house validation in line with commission decision 657/2002/EC
Trends in the occurrence of human and veterinary antibiotics in the sediments of the Yellow River, Hai River and Liao River in northern China
Comprehensive evaluation of antibiotics emission and fate in the river basins of China: source analysis, multimedia modeling, and linkage to bacterial resistance
Simultaneous separation of nitrofuran antibiotics and their metabolites by using micellar electrokinetic capillary chromatography
Determination of pharmaceuticals, iodinated contrast media and musk fragrances in sludge by LC tandem MS and GC/MS
Simultaneous analysis of multiple classes of antibiotics by ion trap LC/MS/MS for assessing surface water and groundwater contamination
Advanced sensing of antibiotics with magnetic gold nanocomposite: electrochemical detection of chloramphenicol
Green preparation of carbon dots for intracellular pH sensing and multicolor live cell imaging
Green preparation of carbon dots with papaya as carbon source for effective fluorescent sensing of Iron (III) and Escherichia coli
Facile one-pot fabrication of Ag@MOF(Ag) nanocomposites for highly selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol in aqueous phase
Lanthanide coordination polymer probe for time-gated luminescence sensing of pH in undiluted human serum
Synthesis of a novel carbazole sulfonohydrazide receptor bearing the 1,8-Naphthalimide units and its anion recognition properties
A nanocluster-based fluorescent sensor for sensitive hemoglobin detection
Highly selective and sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed blue-green photoluminescent carbon nanodots
Highly fluorescent carbon polymer dots prepared at room temperature, and their application as a fluorescent probe for determination and intracellular imaging of ferric ion
A luminescent metal-organic framework with Lewis basic pyridyl sites for the sensing of metal ions
A luminescent Terbium-Succinate MOF thin film fabricated by electrodeposition for sensing of Cu2+ in aqueous environment
A novel highly luminescent LnMOF film: a convenient sensor for Hg2+ detecting
A highly porous luminescent terbium–organic framework for reversible anion sensing
An electrodeposited lanthanide MOF thin film as a luminescent sensor for carbonate detection in aqueous solution
Lanthanide metal–organic frameworks containing a novel flexible ligand for luminescence sensing of small organic molecules and selective adsorption
A facile fabrication of electrodeposited luminescent MOF thin films for selective and recyclable sensing of nitroaromatic explosives
A europium (III) based nano-flake MOF film for efficient fluorescent sensing of picric acid
Recent advances in metal-organic framework-based mixed matrix membranes
Selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol based on a fluorescent nanoscale bis(8-hydroxyquinoline) metal complex
Improvement of inner filter effect correction based on determination of effective geometric parameters using a conventional fluorimeter
Facile, green and clean one-step synthesis of carbon dots from wool: application as a sensor for glyphosate detection based on the inner filter effect
Pb2+ -modified graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for biological aminothiols mediated by an inner filter effect
Highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots for an inner filter effect-based cyanide sensor
A facile synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of picric acid
A turn-on fluorescent sensor for selective and sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with gold nanoclusters based on inner filter effect
Gold nanoclusters as switch-off fluorescent probe for detection of uric acid based on the inner filter effect of hydrogen peroxide-mediated enlargement of gold nanoparticles
On–off–on fluorescent silicon nanoparticles for recognition of chromium(VI) and hydrogen sulfide based on the inner filter effect
Aptasensor for visual and fluorometric determination of lysozyme based on the inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on CdTe quantum dots
Visual and fluorescent assays for selective detection of beta-amyloid oligomers based on the inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles on the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots
Lanthanide aminoisophthalate coordination polymers: a promising system for tunable luminescent properties
Chemical sensors based on amplifying fluorescent conjugated polymers
